speaker: interviewer
text: March 12, 1996. I am the interviewer, Mark Goldberg, interviewing Chief Prosecutor William Dowdell Denson. We're in Lawrence, New York, in the United States. The language is English. This is tape number six. Could you, just in a brief way, summarize your life from the end of the trials to today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came back from Germany in January of 1947. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No, January 1948, I came back. And I was asked to have charge of litigation at the United States Atomic Energy Commission stationed in Washington, DC. And this I did. The Chief Counsel at that time was a man by the name of Adrian Fisher, who was a classmate of mine in Harvard Law School. And I've worked there for the Atomic Energy Commission in charge of their litigation and was there until April of 1952, at which time I left the Atomic Energy Commission and entered private practice in Washington, DC. My office at that time was in the Grange Building, which was just off of Lafayette Square. It's been torn down since then, but I have some very fond memories of my time there. And after I went into private practice, I found that a lot of the people that I had business with were in New York. So in '58 my wife got tired of having a weekend guest for a husband. We moved to New York, to Lawrence, where we are now. And I practice law in New York with Morgan, Finnegan, Durham and Pine. They were engaged in patent litigation, primarily. And that was the type of work that I did until I came out to Mineola and joined Meltzer, Lippe, Goldstein, Wolff, [INAUDIBLE] Schlissel. So that my work basically has been, all my life, in the trial of litigated matters in state and federal courts and for other types of justiciable bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your wife, your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1948 to Constance Ida von Franken Sierstorpff, who was a German citizen and having been born in Germany, but her grandmother was an American. And we have three children. She was married before, and we-- I have a step-daughter, Yvonne, and two children of my own, William and Olivia. They are all grown now, of course. And except for my son, they all have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren to you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask a few more questions about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about the trial experience. Is there anyone that you feel you wanted to try and for whatever reason you didn't get to try? Or did you get to try everyone you felt, within reason, that you needed to try?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the last part of your statement is more accurately reflective of what transpired there. Of course we had 3,500 accused in the war crimes case there at Dachau, and there were an infinite number of those who had participated in the operation of these camps that were never tried. A lot of them are probably alive today. But I think the purpose of these trials was to try to make war more humane, if you please. We tried at Dachau these people, as I stated earlier, for violations of laws and usages of war. And right now there are a lot of potential accused over in Central Europe, in Bosnia and that area. I don't think that anyone can say that what we did at Dachau was useless. We tried to make it apparent to those who violate the laws and usages of law-- war that they would be punished if they did do that. You can never get all of the criminals and try all of the criminals. You only can do so much. But they can be sent a message that if you are going to disobey the, the rules of land warfare and commit war crimes, you can expect to be punished. You may not get it, but you can expect it. And there are a lot of men that are pushing up the flowers right now in the graveyards who had a disregard for humanity. And man's humanity of-- to man has no place in war or in peace. And because of that, I think that, to a certain extent, I can point with pride to what I hope was a contribution to the recognition of the existence of a-- certainly a minimum of morality in the conduct of combatants during the wars that have been waged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any conviction that gave you more satisfaction than the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you talk about conviction, conviction stamps a man. Punishment is when he pays for his conviction. A lot of these people that I tried who were sentenced to be hanged, quite a number of them had their sentences reduced by what was known as a confirming authority in these war crime trials. The lesson is there. It has been taught that if you're going to violate the, the laws, you've got to pay for it. The punishment doesn't always fit the crime, but an effort in that direction is what I think we contributed to at Dachau. I can't say that there was any one individual that was more heinous than the other. All of them stunk. All of them got what they deserved. I don't think there were any of them that received a punishment that was not merited, certainly the punishment that they received was authorized. Now, whether the punishment fit their participation, that's hard to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has the effect of this experience been on you over the last 50 years? How has this affected your work, your life, your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during the trials I lost a lot of weight. As a matter of fact, they recessed Buchenwald and Flossenburg because the court felt that I looked more like an exhibit from the concentration camps than some of the witnesses that I put on the stand. I was skin and bones. I couldn't hold a drink in my hand. I had the shakes. And that was due to the constant pressure that was on me during the course of these trials. I was glad when they were over. The work at the Atomic Energy Commission was extremely interesting. I got to participate in the Rosenberg-Greenglass trials that ended up in their being paying with their lives for their misconduct. But [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it's hard to say that it left an indelible impression on me of any particular type. I've gone to bed and awaken at night thinking about some of it since then. That's another effect on me. How it affected me in the course of my conduct, I don't know, because I don't know what I would have done if I hadn't have done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it a lot in the years--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --years subsequent to the trials.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, only in the last maybe 10 years. I have given talks to various groups, Jewish groups. I've talken-- talked to some high school students because I think it is important that they know what transpired over there and their country's efforts to try to vindicate, if you please, and obtain respect for the law which recognizes human rights. To me, one of the most important things that happened was the recognition that even people who are defeated have rights, and those rights are embodied in treaties that were made when there was no concept of a time that would arise when they would have to invoke those laws. So it was nothing on the spur of the moment. These were efforts to put teeth into the written word, and I think we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anything else you would like to say before we end this tape and put your wife on so you can introduce her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just want to make one remark, and that is that now and then you are told that there's no such thing as the Holocaust and that these things didn't happen. I, of course, did not ever partake of the actual incarceration that these poor individuals were subjected to, and the treatment that they received. But these things happened. And I have not tried to point out to you the horrible nature of the conditions that these people were subjected to. But it did happen. And those that deny it now are those, to me, who are seeking a notoriety and nothing else by saying something that is outlandish. These things did happen, and hopefully they will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. It's been a great honor to interview you. And we'll stop the tape and continue when your wife is here and you can introduce her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Please introduce your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sir. My wife is Countess Constance Ida--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stop. No, stop. He doesn't know all the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sierstorpff-- von Franken Sierstorpff Denson. I married her--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm better know as Huschi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise known as Huschi. I married her in December of 1949, New Year's Eve. And I've been a happily married man ever since that time. She is one of the most learned individuals that I have ever known. There's not but one thing that's wrong, and that is she is hotheaded. She forgives me for what I'm saying, otherwise I wouldn't be saying it because our proximity to each other is such that I could expect a slap in the face if I go too far. For that reason I'm going to stop now. My adorable Huschi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. I always said that I could never win an argument with a lawyer, and so I have a method. I say what I have to say, and then I say no rebuttal and I walk out, because otherwise I lose every time. So we've come to a compromise on that. Anyway, we were married in New Jersey. I had come to the country in '47, and I had met Bill in 1945 in Germany. He was a good friend of a good friend of mine, and he was known as a gentleman, which was really very nice. He was a very polite fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I didn't see him or hear from him again till, I think, March of '49. He called me up in New York because he had seen my brother and my brother gave him my address at the time. So he called up and said, this is Bill Denson. And I say, Bill who? Because a lot of time had passed, and I had lived in France and Switzerland and came here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we met for brunch, and he was half an hour late. So that nearly finished that. But anyway, he persisted and he traveled 11,000 miles and he finally got me to say yes. We were both married before. He didn't have any children, and I had a daughter, which I brought into the marriage. And I think we were married on December 31st because he got two tax deductions. And then we moved to Washington, lived in Washington for eight years. And I really loved Washington, but then his job, unfortunately, took him to New York. So we came here to Long Island. I-- I have relatives here, because my grandmother was Mary Nolton, was an American-- my father's mother. And so I have relatives here. And so I knew about Long Island.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came here and settled here and raised our children. We have three children and five grandchildren. Our son isn't married. Hopefully someday he will.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's been an interesting life. Sometimes he's not the easiest guy to live with, but neither am I. But we really have had a good marriage because we're both, I think, very intelligent people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what effect, if any, have the-- has the experience of the trials had on your marriage? Your family life? Friends? Relationships?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, frankly, the first 20 years or so he really didn't talk about it. I think he wanted to forget it all. Because I was German, and you know, it was sort of a hard subject.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the last maybe-- what, 10, 15 years? It's became more of an issue as time has gone by. And I think I also realized more what his role in history was. We had some difficult times with that. When we were in Washington, we had some death threats from the [INAUDIBLE] family. And we have a pistol permit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that was another reason we didn't want too much publicity while our children grew up. I mean, he hanged 97 Germans. And so I think that, that was sort of a difficult issue, you know, being married to a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that either of you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've enjoyed it very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yeah, we've-- we've had a good life together. He never learned to ski, and I never learned to fish. So I would go skiing with my kids and he would go fish. I tried fishing, but it was no good. But we found golf. And so golf we played very peacefully together, as much as possible. These last few years have been difficult, because Bill has been quite ill. And so he wants to always do more than, health-wise, he should do. That's Bill.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son. He was born in 1950. He looks more like his mother than he looks like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is William Dowdell Denson, Junior.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter Olivia and of me. Olivia was born in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does Olivia do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olivia is a very versatile gal. She [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] is a computer scientist and also is a lover of animals. She has birds and animals in her basement in Parsippany, New Jersey at her home which are unbelievable. You couldn't think that there'd be enough room for humans they are so numerous. This is my daughter Yvonne. She is my stepdaughter, and was born in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. She has three children. So we are grandparents time and time again.

speaker: interviewee
text: You forgot to say that Yvonne has-- that Olivia has two kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: What does she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of Yvonne with her three children. [INAUDIBLE] Katie is the lady that's on one knee in front with Matthew, her brother. And in the rear of Katie is the youngest child--

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexandra.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexandra.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and Yvonne, the mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the mother is, as I said before, Yvonne.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All good looking grandchildren. This is a picture of my granddaughter Tina, who is a daughter of Olivia, and a lovely child. This is a picture of my grandson Eric, who is my daughter Olivia's son and a fine young man. This is a picture of me. It was taken in about 1980 up in the Adirondacks. I am very fond of fly fishing, and I had a good time up there in some water that contained pike, small mouth bass, all of which would take a fly. And I enjoyed it immensely. I wish I looked like that now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
